coredata.dc:title: Two-step synthesis of highly emissive C/ZnO hybridized quantum dots with a broad visible photoluminescence
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Liangjie
Mei, Shiliang
Chen, Qiuhang
Zhang, Wanlu
Zhang, Jie
Zhu, Jiatao
Chen, Guoping
Guo, Ruiqian
coredata.dc:description: In situ growth of ZnO layer on the surface of carbon dots was realized via a two-step method, which resulted in an enhancement of the broad visible emission with a high quantum yield. Influence of the refluxing time, the temperature and the oleylamine/octadecene ratio was investigated to address the key factors on the preparation of the carbon dots. Under the optimal conditions, the carbon dots with an average diameter of 3.4±0.4nm and a photoluminescence quantum yield of 29.3% were achieved. Remarkable improvements of photoluminescence were achieved by the hybridization of the ZnO layer, which can eliminate the surface-trap from the C cores and form the new centers of emission. The synergistic effect arising from the C/ZnO hybridized structure obviously broadened the visible emission and enhanced their photoluminescence quantum yield from 29.3% to 47.3%. The as-prepared highly emissive quantum dots exhibited a broad and stable emission with the Commission Internationaled ‘E’ clairage chromaticity coordinate of (0.23, 0.34), which could offer a promising solution for the future-generation white light emitting diodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ZnO
Hybridized quantum dots
Two-step method
Photoluminescence enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The influence of the refluxing time
The influence of the refluxing temperature
The influence of the OLA/ODE ratio
Morphologies and structure of C/ZnO hybridized QDs
Optical characterization of C/ZnO hybridized QDs
The influence of the concentration of CDs
C/ZnO QDs with enhanced broad fluorescence
The mechanism of the fluorescence enhancement of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis (a) and PL spectra (b) of the CDs prepared at 180°C under different refluxing time from 1h to 5h.
The PL spectra (a) and the normalized PL spectra (b) of the CDs synthesized under different refluxing temperature.
The graphical representation of the synthesis of CDs under different refluxing temperature. Inset: photographs of aqueous solutions of the CDs obtained under UV light (360nm).
(a) PL spectra of CDs prepared at 180°C with different volume ratio of OLA/ODE and (b) the dependence of PL intensity and PL peak position on the OLA/ODE ratio.

Normalized PL spectra of the ZnO QDs synthesized under different refluxing temperature. Inset: Corresponding photographs of dilute ethanolic solution of ZnO QDs with tunable emission obtained under UV irradiation (365nm).
HRTEM images of (a) CDs; (b) size distribution of CDs and (c and d) show the corresponding experimental results for C/ZnO hybridized QDs.
XRD patterns of CDs, ZnO QDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs.
XPS patterns of the CDs (a) and C/ZnO hybridized QDs (b); high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s peaks of CDs (c) and C/ZnO hybridized QDs (d).

(a) The PL spectra of C/ZnO QDs synthesized with different concentration of the CDs ranged from 18mL to 48mL (labeled as A1–A6) (λ ex =360nm). (b) Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates of the six C/ZnO samples. The corresponding CIE coordinates of A1–A6 points are (0.19, 0.24), (0.23, 0.31), (0.22, 0.33), (0.25, 0.36), (0.24, 0.36) and (0.24, 0.38).

The PL spectra of (a) the CDs, ZnO, CDs+ZnO QDs and the C/ZnO hybridized QDs; Inset: photographs of C/ZnO hybridized QDs and CDs dispersed in ethanol under UV (365nm) and sunlight illumination, respectively; (b) CIE color coordinates of the CDs (0.14, 0.11), ZnO QDs (0.24, 0.45) and C/ZnO QDs (0.23, 0.34).
Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves for CDs and C/ZnO QDs (λ ex =360nm).
The formation schematics of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs and the graphical representation of the embryo ZnO wurtzite structures linked to the graphitic layers on the surface of CDs.

head.ce:title: Two-step synthesis of highly emissive C/ZnO hybridized quantum dots with a broad visible photoluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Lighting Technology, Ministry of Education, Institute for Electric Light Sources, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Advanced Lighting Technology, Ministry of Education, Institute for Electric Light Sources, Fudan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Advanced Materials, Fudan University, Shanghai 200438, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Advanced Materials, Fudan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The formation schematics of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs.

In situ growth of ZnO layer on the surface of carbon dots was realized via a two-step method, which resulted in an enhancement of the broad visible emission with a high quantum yield. Influence of the refluxing time, the temperature and the oleylamine/octadecene ratio was investigated to address the key factors on the preparation of the carbon dots. Under the optimal conditions, the carbon dots with an average diameter of 3.4±0.4nm and a photoluminescence quantum yield of 29.3% were achieved. Remarkable improvements of photoluminescence were achieved by the hybridization of the ZnO layer, which can eliminate the surface-trap from the C cores and form the new centers of emission. The synergistic effect arising from the C/ZnO hybridized structure obviously broadened the visible emission and enhanced their photoluminescence quantum yield from 29.3% to 47.3%. The as-prepared highly emissive quantum dots exhibited a broad and stable emission with the Commission Internationaled ‘E’ clairage chromaticity coordinate of (0.23, 0.34), which could offer a promising solution for the future-generation white light emitting diodes.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) have drawn increasing attention in recent years owing to exceptional advantages such as high optical absorptivity, chemical stability, biocompatibility and low toxicity [1–3]. Because of the quantum-confinement effect, the electronic and optical properties of QDs exhibit characteristic dependencies on the nanoparticle sizes and composition, especially the tunable photoluminescence (PL) of the QDs by variation of the nanoparticle sizes [4–6].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) are usually prepared by laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, combustion/thermal method and microwave synthesis [2,7–11]. Despite the versatile synthesis methods, the PL quantum yield (QY) of CDs is often relatively low, which has brought the CDs into a disadvantageous position in various applications. Thus the preparation of highly emissive carbon-based QDs is presently a research area of interest.

Hetero structure formed by the growth of crystalline overlayers on the surface of the QDs offers enhanced emission efficiency [12]. However, besides the high emission efficiency, a broad visible emission from the QDs is also very crucial for many applications such as general lighting. Because the complex monochromatic mixing process is usually not an ideal method, a synergistic solution based on the hetero structure is proposed in this paper to achieve a broad visible emission with a high QY. The C/ZnO hybridized QDs were prepared via a two-step method: (1) Synthesis of CDs with oleylamine (OLA) as a surface capping agent. (2) Growth of ZnO layer on the surface of CDs. The synthesis conditions such as the oleylamine/octadecene (OLA/ODE) ratio, refluxing time, temperature and the concentration of CDs were systematically optimized to investigate their influence on the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. The obtained carbon-based QDs were characterized by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC), X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) and spectrofluorometry. The synergistic effect arising from the C/ZnO hetero structure obviously broadened the visible emission and enhanced their PL QY from 29.3% to 47.3%, for the newly formed ZnO layer effectively passivated the surface defect of CDs. The synergistic effect-induced white-light emission (WLE) from C/ZnO hybridized QDs can offer the opportunity for the future-generation white light emitting diodes [13,14].

All materials used in this work were analytical grade reagents. Zinc acetate, lithium hydroxide monohydrate, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and citric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. OLA (80–90%) and ODE (90%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. The ultrapure water used in all synthesis had a resistivity higher than 18MΩ/cm. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Citric acid (1g) and 0.5M nitric acid (1mL) were added into water (1mL). The mixture was vigorously stirred to dissolve citric acid followed by the injection of 10mL OLA and ODE and the volume ratio of OLA/ODE was ranged from 1:9 to 4:6. The solution was vigorously stirred for 10min to form a milky emulsion followed by refluxing under 170–250°C for 1–5h. The resultant solution was precipitated with methanol, isolated by centrifugation (8000rpm for 5min), and re-dispersed in hexane. These processes were repeated three times to remove residuals. The final colloidal CDs were dried in a vacuum oven (80°C) overnight and then stored in a desiccator for further use.

In a typical synthesis, 1mmol Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 2mmol LiOH·H2O were separately dissolved in 80mL ethanol solution under ultrasonication. Then the precursor solution was prepared by dropwise adding of LiOH solution into Zn(CH3COO)2 solution, which was then refluxed under different temperature ranging from room temperature (RT) to 60°C. After 30min, 50μl TEOS and 100μl pure water were separately injected into the precursor solution followed by refluxing for 5min. After cooling to RT, the resultant solution was precipitated by centrifugation in distilled water. The as-prepared ZnO QDs were re-dispersed in ethanol. These processes were repeated three times to remove residuals. The purified colloidal ZnO QDs were dried in vacuum at 60°C for 24h.

The as-prepared CDs were dispersed in ethanol (30mL) with the aid of ultrasonication for 30min. 5mmol Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O was added to the suspension under vigorous stirring, followed by dropwise adding of an aqueous LiOH solution (6.25mmol). The rest steps were similar to the process of the synthesis of ZnO QDS.

Purified QDs were dispersed in ethanol for UV–vis, PL and HRTEM measurements. The absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded at RT on a Shimadzu UV-3600 ultraviolet spectrophotometer and a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. TEM and HRTEM images were obtained on JEM-2100F HRTEM at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. XPS spectra of the CDs were performed on an ES-CAIAB250 XPS system with Al/Kα as the source and the energy step size was set as 0.100eV. Purified QDs dried in vacuum were used for obtaining XRD patterns on D8 advance with davinci design X-ray diffractometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of the refluxing time
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the refluxing time was studied specially to observe the growth process of the CDs synthesized under 180°C with the OLA/ODE ratio being 3:7. The UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of the CDs synthesized under different refluxing time are shown in Fig. 1a and b. It can be seen that the absorbance and luminescence intensity depend strongly on the refluxing time. After 1h, the first exciton absorption peak centered at 370nm attributing to n-π* transition can be observed indicating the formation of the CDs. After another 1h heating, a red shift from 370nm to 378nm can be observed at the UV–vis spectra owing to the growth of the CDs. With the further increase of the refluxing time to 3h or more, the first exciton absorption peak of the CDs becomes broader and more asymmetric, and the second absorption peak centered at 270nm attributing to π-π* transition emerges as shown in Fig. 1a [15]. That is, the longer the refluxing time, the more carbonization or/and aggregation, which results in the disappearance of the n-π* transition and the consequent shift to the π-π* transition. It can also be seen that both the absorbance and the PL spectra under different refluxing time present a maximum intensity at 1.5h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of the refluxing temperature
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the influence of the refluxing temperature, CDs were synthesized under different refluxing temperature ranging from 170 to 250°C with the OLA/ODE ratio being 3:7 and the refluxing time being 1.5h, whose PL spectra and normalized PL spectra are shown in Fig. 2a and b. It can be seen that the PL spectra of the CDs obtained under the refluxing temperature less than 220°C shows a blue emission peak centered at 440nm, which remains constant with the variation of the refluxing temperature ranging from 170°C to 220°C. It can also be seen that the PL spectra present a maximum intensity at 180°C. However, with the refluxing temperature increased to 250°C, the PL spectrum consists of a blue emission peak centered at 440nm and a green emission peak at 516nm. Fig. 3 shows the graphical representation of the synthesis of the CDs under different refluxing temperature and the photographs of aqueous solutions of the CDs obtained under UV light (360nm). It can be seen that under a lower refluxing temperature, the hydroxyl groups are formed around the surface of the CDs which contributes to the strong blue emission. However under a higher refluxing temperature, the oxidization of the hydroxyl groups by nitric acid is promoted and the amount of CO bonds on the surface of the CDs can be obviously increased, which leads to the appearance of the weak green emission. That is, the green emission is attributed to the existence of the CO bonds while the existence of the CO bonds contributes to the blue emission [16,17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of the OLA/ODE ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CDs are stabilized by OLA as an emulsifier. To achieve complete passivation state of the CDs, relative to the amount of carbon precursor, enough surface passivation agent (OLA) is necessary. With the OLA and ODE mixture solution volume being constant (10mL), CDs were synthesized by variation of the ratio of OLA/ODE from 4:6 to 1:9 under 180°C for 1.5h. Fig. 4 a exhibits the influence of the OLA/ODE ratio on the PL intensity of CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 4b that there is an obvious linear increase relationship between the OLA/ODE ratio and the PL intensity when the OLA/ODE ratio drops from 4:6 to 3:7. With the ratio further decreased to 1:9, the linear decrease relationship between the OLA/ODE ratio and the PL intensity is also observed. Thus it can be concluded that the optimum ratio is 3:7. In addition, it can be observed that the fluorescence emission presents a red shift from 435nm to 453nm, which indicates that the decrease of the OLA/ODE ratio favors the growth of the CDs with larger size, which may be attributed to the fact that too much OLA molecules lead to early termination of the growth of the CDs and too little OLA molecules cannot passivate the surface of the CDs and thus the aggregation between the CDs occurs.

To achieve a broad visible photoluminescence from the C/ZnO hybridized QDs, the yellowgreen emission from the ZnO shell is essential besides the blue emission from the CDs. It is found that the refluxing temperature plays an important role in controlling the tunable emission of ZnO QDs. Other experimental conditions and the synthesis process have been described in Section 2.3. The normalized PL spectra of the as-synthesized ZnO QDs under different refluxing temperature ranging from 25 to 60°C for 0.5h are measured and shown in Fig. 5 . The PL spectrum of the sample synthesized at 25°C exhibits a broad and strong emission centered at 440nm. With the further increase of the refluxing temperature to 50°C, a red shift from 440nm to 531nm can be observed at the PL spectra owing to the growth of ZnO QDs. However, the emission peak position almost remains constant with the variation of the refluxing temperature ranging from 50°C to 60°C. According to the S.A. Studenikin's report [18], the amount of oxygen vacancies is related to the yellowgreen emssion of ZnO QDs, and the higher temperature, the more opportunities of forming Oi or OZn, which contributes to the red shift from blue to yellowgreen emission. However at temperatures higher than 50°C, the further formation of Oi or OZn is limited with the depletion of the hydroxyl groups in the solution which completely react with zinc precursor, and thus leads to the slight change in the yellowgreen emission band.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and structure of C/ZnO hybridized QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a and c shows the HRTEM images of the CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs, and the corresponding size distribution data are shown in Fig. 6b and d. It can be seen from Fig. 6a and b that the CDs appear as isolated quasi-spherical particles in shape and the diameter of CDs is 3.4±0.4nm. The enlarged view in Fig. 6a reveals that the CDs are highly crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.25nm (100), which suggests that the CDs are graphitic structure. It can be seen from Fig. 6c and d that the C/ZnO hybridized QDs with a diameter of about 5.2nm also appear nearly spherical in shape. The enlarged view in Fig. 6c clearly shows the appearance of two different crystal phases which reveals that the outer layer on the surface of the CDs is actually composed of ZnO with a lattice spacing of 0.26nm (002). Furthermore, the ZnO layer with an estimated thickness of approximately 1.8nm is clearly observed.

Fig. 7 presents the XRD patterns of the as-prepared CDs, ZnO QDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs. For the C/ZnO hybridized QDs, it can be seen in Fig. 7 that the characteristic diffraction peak centered at 2θ =25.8° attributing to C (002) matches well with typical graphitic phase, suggesting the CDs with graphitic structure were synthesized after pyrolysis process. The XRD peaks tend to be broadened because of the size effect. According to the Scherrer Equation, the crystallite size of the synthesized CDs, estimated from the (002) peak width, is about 3.4nm which agrees well with the HRTEM result. In addition, the intense peaks of ZnO (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) can be seen in the XRD pattern of C/ZnO hybridized QDs. The positions and intensity ratio of these peaks agree well with those of standard ZnO bulk (JCPDS no. 36-1451), which proves the existence of ZnO layer with wurtzite structure on the surface of CDs.

The chemical compositions and structures of the CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs are investigated by XPS analysis. The full XPS spectra of the CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs are shown in Fig. 8 a and b. It can be seen that the CDs are mainly composed of C, O, and N. Elemental analysis also confirms the composition of these two kinds of QDs (CDs: C 85.7wt%, N 3.51wt%, O 10.78wt%; C/ZnO QDs: C 44.37wt%, N 1.89wt%, O 34.57wt%, Zn 19.17wt%). The ratio indicates that C/ZnO hybridized QDs have a larger amount of oxygen than CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s peaks in Fig. 8c and d reveals four carbon atom peaks at 284.7eV, 285.6eV, 286.0eV, and 287.4eV, which are assigned to the carbon of CC/CC, CN, CO and CO/CN, respectively. These bonds mostly stem from carbogenic cores, surface-bound OLA molecules and surface carbonyl groups. Noticeably, the energy peak at 284.7eV is ascribed to C atoms in graphitic structure. From Fig. 8c and d, it can be seen that the content of CO bond for C/ZnO hybridized QDs is 4.17%, higher than that for CDs (2.27%), which indicates the new formation of CO bond on the CDs after capped with ZnO layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of C/ZnO hybridized QDs
The influence of the concentration of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CDs under 180°C in Section 3.1.2 were used as the seed for the in situ growth of ZnO layer on the surface of CDs via a two-step method. With the fixed amount of the injected zinc precursors, we varied the amount of the ethanolic solution of the CDs from 18mL to 48mL with the total solution volume being constant to study the influence of the concentration of CDs. Fig. 9 a shows a series of PL spectra of C/ZnO hybridized QDs synthesized with different concentration of CDs ranged from 18mL to 48mL (labeled as A1–A6). The PL spectra of C/ZnO hybridized QDs are composed of two parts by the enhanced blue and yellowgreen emission centered at 440nm and 531nm respectively, and their compositional ratio varies with the concentration of CDs, which can be regarded as a signature of growth of the ZnO layer on the surface of CDs and which also enables us to realize the white-light emission from the C/ZnO hybridized QDs. Fig. 9b shows the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates of the six C/ZnO samples (A1–A6). As can be seen, the emitted light from the C/ZnO QDs synthesized under different amount of the CDs ethanolic solution which is 42mL and 36mL (A2 and A3) is close to the scope of white-light emission. In addition, when the amount of CDs reaches 36mL (A3), the PL intensity reaches the maximum as shown in Fig. 9a. While too much (48mL) or too little (18mL) amount of CDs cannot favor the formation of the ZnO layer on the surface of CDs and thus the PL intensity drops obviously. Accordingly, A1 and A6 are far away from the region of white light area as shown in Fig. 9b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C/ZnO QDs with enhanced broad fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 10 a shows the PL spectra of the CDs, ZnO QDs, CDs+ZnO QDs and the C/ZnO hybridized QDs and the photographs of C/ZnO hybridized QDs and CDs dispersed in ethanol under UV (365nm) and daylight illumination, respectively. It can be seen that the as-prepared CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs solution are colorless and transparent under daylight. The as-prepared CDs only exhibit blue fluorescence under UV excitation. After capped by ZnO layer, the obtained C/ZnO hybridized QDs exhibit bright white emission under UV light, which is consistent with the broad emission almost covering the entire visible range (from 380 to 700nm) in the PL spectrum of the C/ZnO QDs in Fig. 10a. CIE chromaticity coordinate can be calculated from this emission curve by a software GoCIE. The color coordinate is determined to be (0.23, 0.34), just belonging to the white gamut, as shown in Fig. 10b. It is concluded that the synergistic effect arising from the C/ZnO hybridized QDs structure obviously broadens the visible emission. In addition, the C/ZnO hybridized QDs can eliminate the surface defect of the CDs and give rise to a great enhancement of fluorescence as shown in Fig. 10a with a PL QY increased from 29.3% (CDs) to 47.3% (C/ZnO QDs), which is also higher than that of ZnO QDs (13%). The PL QY is evaluated using the following equation:
(1)
Q x
=
Q
s t d
F x
F
s t d
A
s t d
A x
n x 2
n
s t d
2
where Q, F, A and n denote PL QY, the integral PL intensity, and the optical density and reflective index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts x and std indicate the parameters for the specimen and standard sample, respectively. A quinine sulphate solution in 0.1M H2SO4 with the literature QY of 54.7% at 360nm is used as a standard. To minimize the re-absorption effect, we maintain the absorbance of the sample solution less than 0.1 by dilution.
n x 2
=
n
s t d
2
is approximately equal to 1.

To acquire further insight into the photoluminescence, the time-resolved fluorescence of CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs were performed under the excitation of 360nm. As seen in Fig. 11 , both CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs exhibit exponential decay. The calculated average lifetimes for CDs and C/ZnO hybridized QDs are 6.68 and 8.16ns, respectively. As we know, diverse fluorophores or energy levels in the samples are responsible for their multiple lifetimes. Thus, relative to CDs, the significantly increased lifetime of C/ZnO hybridized QDs implies decreased nonradiative traps and increased fluorophore density, which may be closely associated with the surface passivation by ZnO layer [1,3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the fluorescence enhancement of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence mechanism of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs with the enhanced broad fluorescence is not fully understood. According to the above results, a possible mechanism for the fluorescence enhancement of the C/ZnO QDs can be proposed. Fig. 12 shows the formation schematics of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs and the graphical representation of the embryo ZnO wurtzite structures linked to the graphitic layers on the surface of CDs. The origin of fluorescence in the C/ZnO hybridized QDs can be generally classified into two main categories: C-related blue emission and ZnO-related yellowgreen emission. Based on the above XPS results, the content of the CO bonds increases with the CDs capped with ZnO layer. The newly formed CO bonds decrease the nonradiative recombination, which could contribute to the enhanced PL emission of the C/ZnO hybridized QDs, that is, the surface state of the CDs has great effect on their PL QY [18,19]. The ZnO layer comes from the possible chemical reactions between Zn2+ and the functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy groups) on the surface of the CDs. ZnO exhibits the tunable emission in the visible spectral region, which is originated from the different defect centers such as zinc vacancy (VZn), zinc interstitial (Zni), oxygen vacancy (VO), oxygen interstitial (Oi) and antisite oxygen (OZn) [1,20–23]. The newly formed CO bonds linked to the graphitic layers favor the formation of more Oi defects which can lead to the yellowgreen emission of ZnO.

In summary, highly emissive C/ZnO hybridized QDs with a broad visible photoluminescence can be synthesized via a two-step method. Fluorescence enhancement with the PL QY increased from 29.3% to 47.3% can be realized by the synergistic effect arising from the C/ZnO hetero structure, that is, the CDs can be passivated by ZnO layers and the nonradiative recombination are decreased. The PL spectra of C/ZnO hybridized QDs consist of the enhanced blue emission from the CDs and the tunable yellowgreen emission from the ZnO layer, which enables us to control their compositional ratio for the white-light emission from the C/ZnO hybridized QDs. The concentration of CDs and the refluxing temperature are identified as two key parameters for obtaining the high quality white-light emission. The as-prepared C/ZnO hybridized QDs show high PL QY, low toxicity and broad visible emission, which are promising as versatile light emitting materials suitable for various applications ranging from solid-state lighting to display.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 61377046, NSFC, No. 61144010, NSFC, No. 60606018, and NSFC, No. 61177021).
